data.title: AMD: Expensive For A Reason, Upgrade Before Earnings
data.description:
It appears that timing of my previous neutral AMD (AMD) thesis was quite good as the share price remained at approximately the same level since November while the S&P 500 Index delivered a 1.6% increase. AMD is set to release its Q4 earnings next week, on February 3rd after the market close. While AMD's valuation remains stretched since there were no notable positive earnings revisions recently, I think that there might be new ones after the upcoming earnings release as I expect Lisa Su to speak about soaring demand for AI accelerators during the earnings call. Recent earnings of the two large data center investors, Microsoft and Meta, clearly showed that hyperscalers do not plan to cut AI capital investments in the foreseeable future. Moreover, we see that China is set to resume buying chips from the U.S. giants, which is another potential large growth driver for AMD. Therefore, I believe that AMD's elevated multiples are justified and the stock deserves an upgrade to 'Buy' despite being the laggard compared to Nvidia (NVDA). The addressable market is expanding that fast so it will be enough space for both NVDA and AMD to capitalize on the secular trend.

Q4 earnings preview

First and foremost, AMD is very strong in beating consensus estimates almost every quarter. The company's earnings history suggests that there were only three EPS misses and only one revenue miss over the last four years, which means that the win rate is high. The win rate alone doesn't mean that a new double earnings beat is guaranteed on February 3rd, but it underscores the management commitment to deliver on its financial targets. Moreover, there are robust fundamental factors supporting the expectation of a solid double earnings beat next week.

Wall Street analysts are optimistic, which I see from the recent earnings revisions setup outlined above. Analysts forecast AMD's Q4 revenue to amount to $9.67 billion, which will be a 26% year-over-year increase. The EPS is expected to deliver a 20%+ growth as well by increasing from $1.09 to $1.32. These projected increases look quite conservative given AMD's strong growth momentum in three previous quarters when the company maintained a 30%+ revenue growth.

Based on recent developments in the industry, I tend to agree with Wall Street's optimism. Microsoft and Meta released their Q4 earnings yesterday, and they were marked with each company's management's ambitious plans regarding expanding their AI compute capacity. Microsoft demonstrated impressive revenue growth in cloud business and substantial Copilot adoption during the quarter. The management reiterated a strategic emphasis on AI by broadening its ecosystem of offerings, which is likely to support elevated CapEx investments. And Microsoft's management is confident that aggressive investments in AI infrastructure will payoff because CFO Amy Hood stated, 'the majority of the capital that we're spending today... are already contracted for most of their useful life'.

Meta's management also sees value in their aggressive investments in AI. During the earnings call, Mark Zuckerberg cited 'AI-driven performance gains' and there was a massive FY2026 capital expenditures guidance between $115 billion and $135 billion. The company's CapEx outlook is notably higher compared to guidance provided in Q3, which means that Meta shifts to even more aggressive AI infrastructure investment. Since both MSFT and META power their data centers with AMD's EPYC processors, yesterday's earnings calls are certainly bullish for the company. Therefore, I expect AMD to not only deliver another double beat next week, but I also expect the management to release an upbeat FY2026 guidance.

Moreover, let's not forget that there is another large catalyst on the horizon, which is the strong demand for high-end American chips from Chinese tech giants. During the previous earnings call, AMD's management spoke about Chinese market with caution because at that time the level of geopolitical uncertainty was still quite high. However, today this uncertainty does not look that bad because in December Lisa Su confirmed that AMD has licenses to ship its MI308 chips to China. Of course, there is competition in China from the local chip manufacturers, but the fact that Chinese government recently gave nod to the country's tech giants to order Nvidia's H200 processors suggests that the demand is too strong so local suppliers are unable to meet it either from the volume perspective or from computing power perspective. Therefore, I think that AMD can capitalize on soaring demand in China as well.

Mitigating factors

It is quite difficult to predict whether there will be a strong positive reaction from investors post-earnings as AMD's current valuation is stretched, meaning that big optimism is already priced in. The company's estimated FY2028 forward P/E ratio looks quite good at 18, but to achieve such dramatic P/E contraction AMD needs to deliver aggressive EPS growth over several years ahead.

While there are extremely strong AI tailwinds that will support AMD's growth, it is crucial to keep in mind that AMD is not the biggest fish in the pond. Nvidia is a much stronger player based on its scale and financial power, which means that AMD will highly likely be able to capitalize only on what is left after Nvidia takes its large piece of the pie. The demand is so strong at the moment and Nvidia doesn't have enough capacity to capture even a larger share of the market. However, with their $60 billion cash pile (~9 times larger than AMD's) and $53 billion TTM free cash flow (~18 times larger than AMD's), it is in a much stronger financial position to invest in growth. Therefore, over the long-term there is a significant risk of Nvidia significantly boosting its capacity to conquer a larger market share at AMD's expense.

Moreover, there is extremely high level of uncertainty regarding the longevity of the current data center spending spree. Of course, tech behemoths will continue investing in AI infrastructure until AI adoption is strong and they see robust revenue growth and profitability expansion due to cross-selling AI capabilities. However, it is impossible to forecast for how long their AI-related revenues will soar and justify aggressive data center spending.

Conclusion

I think that we are likely to see a solid earnings release from AMD next week with a double beat on headline numbers coupled with an upbeat guidance based on soaring demand for AMD's data center business. After a strong earnings release there might be a big share price movement, but I am not ready to give AMD a 'Strong buy' rating because over the long haul there is a huge competition risk posed by Nvidia. Therefore, my take is that AMD is a 'Buy' at the moment.

data.title: ONEOK: My Top 3 Reasons To Buy The Stock
data.description:
Introduction

ONEOK (NYSE: OKE) has been a great performer since I rated it a Strong Buy back in late October, outperforming the market by more than 15%. With a lot of great choices in the energy landscape, particularly in the midstream industry, I want to go through my best reasons for why the company is a great choice for investors.

To give a quick overview to readers, ONEOK is heavily focused on natural gas with a Gathering and Processing segment as well as NGLs and a natural gas pipeline segment. Operations span through various regions such as the mid-continent and the Rocky Mountain regions.

Reason #1: The Wellhead-to-Water Strategy

Basically, ONEOK is following its master plan to stop being just a middleman and start being a fully integrated energy toll booth. Before 2024, the company would hand off products to other companies to reach final export markets, but now it is aiming to capture every inch of the journey. Through the acquisition of both Medallion and EnLink, OKE has now secured the first touch on energy molecules. For example, EnLink added 1.7 Bcf/d of Permian gas-processing capacity, allowing OKE to strip out valuable NGLs such as ethane and propane at the source rather than paying someone else to do it.

Through these, the product is moved through the 60,000-mile network to the super hub Mont Belvieu in Texas. The rebuild of the Medford fractionator is also hitting major milestones, with Phase 1 expected to come online in Q4 to reach a capacity of 100,000 bpd, substantially lowering the cost of separating NGLs from 20 cents to 10 cents a gallon by the end of 2026. This will also be paired with synergies from the deals now projected to exceed $500MM.

The final link is also part of the new ONEOK identity, and this would simply be water, as they are no longer staying inland. OKE partnered with MPLX (MPLX) to build a 400,000 bpd LPG export terminal in Texas City, which is expected to be fully operational by 2028, allowing them to sell Permian propane and butane directly to international markets in Asia and Europe. OKE is also building the Eiger Express pipeline that will move 2.5 Bcf/d from the Permian to Corpus Christi LNG export hubs, which will become the interstate highway connecting the wellhead to the global gas market.

This is super important for investors to grasp as it shifts OKE from a volume-dependent company to a margin-capture company. It also signals to us that OKE can now sign direct-supply contracts with utility companies building gas-fired plants for data centers. They can guarantee physical delivery of gas because they own the entire chain of supply, and hyperscalers will be willing to pay a premium for this.

Reason #2: The AI Power Surge

AI has basically become a massive physical infrastructure boom that pushes the company to have absolute reliability. AI workloads require 24/7 uninterrupted power, and solar & wind are intermittent, while batteries aren’t yet scaled to power a 500MW data center through, let’s say, a three-day cloudy stretch. ONEOK is then well-positioned to capture the required fuel lines for these new AI power plants, as OKE is heavily focused on natural gas.

The company is also well-positioned in the Sun Belt, an area primed for data center construction thanks to lower regulations and lower building costs. And that’s exactly where the company’s 60,000 miles of pipes are buried. Time to power has become the number 1 metric for data center developers, as it can take 5 to 7 years to get a grid connection from a utility. But a developer can build an off-grid natural gas power plant in a maximum of 24 months if they are sitting on a ONEOK pipeline. And now, as ONEOK owns the gathering lines in the Permian, the company can offer direct-from-source gas to data centers being built in West Texas, bypassing the congested main power grid entirely. The company is already capitalizing on behind-the-meter infrastructure by building lateral pipelines that are short but high-margin, connecting main interstate lines directly to a specific data center campus.

The data center paradigm is making the EIA forecast the strongest four-year growth in US power demand since 2000, which has made analysts revise their thesis on energy demand that will require an additional 3 to 6 Bcf/d of natural gas by 2030. Given OKE’s position in the Midcontinent and Permian, it is well-positioned to capture that new demand simply by being the incumbent infrastructure provider.

Reason #3: Valuation

Despite the most recent rally, ONEOK is relatively cheap compared to its peers, due in part to its sharp relative drop in 2025, which made it one of the most hated energy stocks. In 2026, I believe that we can see a coiled spring type of action. First, the company had a sort of fear factor priced in with its debt level coming in from the acquisition of EnLink and Medallion, but now that the integrations are finished, the current fear factor is evaporating, leaving behind a massive and highly profitable company trading at a clearance bin price. Right now, it has an EV/EBITDA of 11.07, well below Williams Companies (WMB) at 17.41 and Kinder Morgan (KMI) at 13.81, while it posted the strongest growth over the past 3 years and arguably the best prospects. Historically speaking, the company is also trading below its average EV/EBITDA despite having better prospects ahead.

OKE is also now generating strong free cash flows, and with the current price, this translates to a healthy FCF yield of 6%. I expect this to rise thanks to new synergies numbers that are expected to reach $500MM, which would translate into pure profit. More so, the company currently rewards investors incredibly well with its current dividend yield of 5.28% that does not need the hassles of MLPs and K-1s.

Furthermore, I believe that the market has not yet fully priced in the cash flow impact of the estimated $1.5B in tax savings coming in from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act that will be realized over the next few years. The company will be able to use accelerated depreciation and other specific perks to essentially stop paying deferred cash taxes for the next several years. Meaning that OKE can further ramp up its FCF generation, which can pay down debt much faster than Wall Street predicted.

Though the company’s current leverage ratio is too high for some investors at 4.4x, this should drastically help the company, and management has guided to hitting that 3.5x target by late 2026, which could push for a re-rating from credit agencies, further boosting the appeal to invest in ONEOK and its profitability profile with smaller interest payments.

Risks

Outside the company’s large debt, the company is facing a 2026 LNG glut as the world is being flooded with natural gas, with tonnes of projects coming online simultaneously in the US, Qatar, and Canada. And though, despite natural gas currently surging due to the winter storm, prices are expected to soften significantly in late 2026. Though ONEOK is currently insulated from these, as it gets paid for the volume in the pipe, producers might reduce their flows, and empty pipes don’t pay tolls.

And though ONEOK is currently operating in a relatively friendly federal environment, state-level regulations are currently tightening. States like Colorado are implementing stricter methane emission rules and local permitting hurdles. These regulations could delay the specific lateral pipeline projects ONEOK needs to serve those new AI data centers.

Conclusion

ONEOK is currently one of the best growth opportunities in the midstream industry, especially as it is heavily focused on natural gas, which presents the best growth opportunities right now with the AI boom. I believe that the company will easily beat expectations regarding its debt levels, and the ongoing tailwinds will boost its FCF generation; thus, the current dividend yield gives a great opportunity cost for investors. I keep my Strong Buy rating on ONEOK.

data.title: Whale's Market Outlook 2026: Liquidity, Expectations And 'The New Order'
data.description:
For global investors, 2025 was one of the most undercurrent-filled years of the 21st century. Unlike the bursting of the dot-com bubble in 2001 or the global financial crisis in 2008, markets in 2025 did not experience a prolonged, large-scale liquidation cycle or a “storm-like” sequence of relentless crashes. Yet it is clear that, amid geopolitical uncertainty, uncertainty over US fiscal and monetary policy, uncertainty across multiple countries’ economic fundamentals, and the ebbing of globalisation in favour of regionalisation, equities, bonds, commodities and crypto have all been pricing in a future that is more cautious and more defensive. Against that backdrop, liquidity allocation has become less concentrated in equities and bonds than it once was. Commodities, FX and rates attracted greater attention in 2025. At the same time, investors have been steadily reducing leverage and trimming exposure to higher-risk assets—one of the direct reasons the crypto bull market ended in Q4 2025. So, where do markets go in 2026? As in 2025, implied expectations embedded in derivatives-market data have already offered an answer.

Liquidity: Not Abundant

At the start of 2025, one major “bullish” factor in investors’ minds was Donald Trump’s formal inauguration. The prevailing view was that Trump would trigger more rate cuts, inject more liquidity into markets, and drive asset prices higher.

Indeed, between September and December 2025, amid “concerns about a weakening labour market”, the Federal Reserve delivered three “defensive” rate cuts and, in December, announced the end of quantitative tightening. But this did not produce the liquidity flood investors had hoped for. From October 2025 onwards, the Effective Federal Funds Rate (EFFR) gradually moved towards the midpoint of the “rate corridor”. In the following months, EFFR crossed that midpoint and drifted towards the upper bound of the corridor—hardly a sign of easy liquidity.

EFFR is the core short-term market rate in the US. It reflects funding liquidity conditions in the banking system and how the Fed’s policy stance (hikes or cuts) is transmitted in practice. In relatively loose liquidity regimes, EFFR tends to sit closer to the lower end of the corridor, as banks have less need for frequent overnight borrowing. In the final months of 2025, however, banks clearly faced liquidity tightness—a key driver of the rise in EFFR.

The SOFR–IORB spread further highlights the degree of stress. If EFFR primarily reflects cash-market conditions, SOFR, secured funding collateralised by US Treasury securities, captures a broader liquidity shortage. Since October 2025, SOFR has remained above the Interest Rate on Reserve Balances (IORB), indicating that banks have been willing to pay a higher rate premium to “bid” for liquidity.

Notably, even after the Fed stopped shrinking its balance sheet, the SOFR–IORB spread did not fall sharply in January. One plausible explanation is that, during 2025, banks deployed a significant share of their liquidity buffers into financial investments rather than extending credit to the commercial, industrial, and real estate sectors. Over the past year, commercial and industrial lending contracted meaningfully versus 2024, and consumer credit showed similar weakness. By contrast, VettaFi data suggest that margin debt rose 36.3% over the past year, reaching an all-time high of $1.23T in December 2025, while investors’ net debit balances also expanded to $ -814.1 billion—broadly matching the pace of margin debt growth.

As liquidity requirements grow to push markets higher, the banking system is showing signs of strain, and demand for short-term funding has increased. The fix is straightforward: either reduce margin lending and pull liquidity back, or obtain liquidity support from the Fed and the repo market. For the economy as a whole, the first option is preferable—lower system-wide leverage and strengthen resilience in banks and the financial system—but it would also imply lower valuations and a sharp equity sell-off. Given the midterm-election backdrop, the White House is unlikely to accept that path. As a result, in 2025 alone, the repo market expanded from roughly $6T to more than $12.6T—over three times its size during the 2021 bull market. In 2026, repo may need to expand further to support equity-market performance.

Repo transactions typically use US Treasuries—“high-quality assets”—as collateral. Historically, Treasury notes (T-notes) have been the most important form of collateral. But since mid-2023, that has changed, in part because the issuance and outstanding stock of Treasury bills (T-bills) has increased in an “exponential” fashion. This is not benign: a rising share of T-bills in total government debt often signals deteriorating sovereign credit perceptions. As investors begin to doubt a government’s repayment capacity, they may become less willing to buy long-dated bonds at relatively low yields. To reduce debt-servicing pressure, the government leans more heavily on T-bill financing—raising the T-bill share further and reinforcing investor doubts in a vicious cycle.

A higher T-bill share has another consequence: liquidity dynamics become less stable. Since a large portion of the liquidity supporting equities is channelled via repo, a greater reliance on T-bills implies more frequent rollovers and a shorter average liquidity “life”. With overall leverage and margin debt already pushing beyond historical peaks, more frequent and more violent liquidity swings weaken the market’s shock-absorption capacity—setting the stage for potential cascading liquidations and large price moves.

In short: the quality of USD liquidity deteriorated markedly in 2025, with no clear sign of improvement so far.

So, in this macro context, how have investors’ expectations and portfolios changed?

Risk Premia and “Strict Diversification”

One cost of poorer-quality USD liquidity is that USD-based long-term funding costs remain elevated. This is intuitive: as USD asset markets become more fragile, US Treasury debt expands sharply (reaching USD 38.5 trillion by December 2025), and US fiscal, monetary and foreign policy turn more uncertain and less predictable, the perceived probability of systemic risk rises over time—prompting long-term Treasury investors to demand greater compensation. Since long-term financing rates are typically anchored to the 10-year Treasury yield, it is telling that the 10-year yield fell only 31 bps over the past year—far less than the 75 bps decline in policy rates. This implies long-term funding costs stayed above 4%.

High funding costs constrain positioning. When a risk asset’s implied forward return falls below Treasury yields, holding that risk asset long-term becomes unattractive. Crypto is a textbook example: as implied forward returns declined, investors progressively reduced exposure, and the market moved into a bearish phase.

Compared with expensive long-term liquidity, short-term liquidity funded via T-bills is materially cheaper. But T-bill funding is also short-duration, creating an environment naturally favourable to speculation: investors can borrow short, apply high leverage, push prices up quickly and exit. Markets may look buoyant in the short run, but speculative froth makes rallies difficult to sustain—something clearly visible in the liquidity-sensitive crypto market.

Meanwhile, after decades, “strict diversification” made a comeback in 2025. Unlike the traditional 60/40 approach, liquidity has been spread across a broader set of instruments rather than confined to USD assets. In fact, throughout 2025, investors steadily reduced the share of USD and USD-pegged assets in portfolios. Although persistent net outflows did not visibly hit US equities, incremental liquidity was allocated more heavily to non-US markets. Assets tightly pegged to USD or USD-denominated leverage (crypto, WTI oil, the dollar itself) underperformed, while assets less tied to the dollar (such as precious metals) delivered far stronger performance than other major asset classes.

Notably, simply holding euros or Swiss francs performed no worse than holding the S&P 500. This suggests a profound shift in investor logic—one that goes beyond a single business cycle.

The New Order

What most deserves reassessment in 2026 is not a linear question like “will growth be stronger?”, but rather the fact that markets are adopting a new pricing grammar. Over the past two decades, returns often rested on two implicit assumptions: first, supply chains were organised around maximum efficiency, suppressing costs and stabilising inflation; second, central banks provided powerful backstops during crises, systematically compressing risk premia. Both assumptions are now weakening. Supply chains increasingly prioritise control and redundancy; fiscal and industrial policy appears more frequently in profit models; and geopolitics has shifted from tail risk to constant noise. “Regionalisation” is less a slogan than a change in the constraint set facing the global economic system.

In this framework, the key is not to bet on a single direction, but to realign exposures to three more reliable “hard variables”: supply constraints, capital expenditure, and policy-driven order flow. Together, they point towards a set of assets: commodity-linked equities, the AI infrastructure chain, defence and security themes, and select non-US markets that improve portfolio correlation structures. At the same time, the core question in rates and government bonds is no longer “how much tailwind will rate cuts bring?”, but how the new term structure reshapes the distribution of returns.

Regionalisation: Not “Decoupling”, but a New Cost Function

Equating “regionalisation” with “full decoupling” tends to understate its true impact. A more accurate description is that globalisation’s objective function has shifted from “efficiency at all costs” to “efficiency under security constraints”. Once security becomes a binding constraint, many variables that previously sat outside valuation models—supply-chain redundancy, energy security, access to critical minerals, export controls on key technologies, and the rigidity of defence budgets—begin to enter discount rates and earnings expectations in various forms.

This produces two direct consequences for asset pricing. First, risk premia become less likely to revert to structurally low levels: political and policy uncertainty becomes an everyday variable, and markets require greater compensation. After all, nobody wants to bear “Cuban equity risk”, and today, even in US equities, that “Cuban equity risk” is no longer zero.

Second, global beta explains less, while regional alpha matters more: under different blocs and policy functions, the same growth and the same inflation can produce very different valuations and capital flows. For allocators, diversification in the age of regionalisation looks less like splitting assets evenly by country and more like diversifying across supply-chain position and policy elasticity.

Equities: From “Buying Growth” to “Buying Location”

If 2010–2021 equity allocation was largely about “buying growth and falling discount rates”, 2026 is more about “buying location”. “Location” refers to where a market sits on three maps: the resource map, the compute map and the security map. As the world emphasises supply-chain autonomy and critical infrastructure security, markets positioned at key nodes are more likely to earn a structural premium, even if their domestic macro picture is imperfect.

In an era where security is the top priority, increasing inventories of gold, silver, copper and other non-ferrous metals can be rational even if they are not immediately needed. Supply chains can be disrupted without warning (as last year’s trade tensions showed), sharply raising costs and forcing major countries to hold larger mineral reserves against potential shocks. Structurally rising demand for critical minerals, combined with long-cycle supply constraints, makes commodities behave more like “supply-side assets” than mere mirrors of the traditional business cycle. Options-market implied expectations reflect this: although investors see signs of overheating in some non-ferrous metals markets (particularly silver), traders still anticipate further upside potential for gold over the longer run.

This logic also provides a clearer allocation case for equities in resource-rich countries. Copper-linked equities—Chile is a prime example—partly reflect foundational shifts in electrification and in demand for industrial infrastructure. Precious-metals resource equities—South Africa is a typical case—combine commodity upside with the double-edged nature of risk premia: when commodities rise, profits and the currency may reinforce each other; when risk rises, politics and external financing conditions can amplify volatility. For portfolio construction, resource-country equities are better understood as a “supply-constraint factor” than simply emerging-market beta.

Another central theme is AI. AI discussions are easily pulled towards application-layer narratives, but allocators should focus on balance-sheet realities: compute, energy, data centres, networks, and cooling. These links share two traits: higher capex visibility and often benefit from joint support from policy and industry. Rather than treating AI as another software-valuation game, it may be more robust to view it as a new wave of infrastructure build-out. Higher compute density ultimately translates into greater power and engineering demand, shifting more of the return distribution upstream and into midstream “real-economy” segments.

Under regionalisation, computing infrastructure is also spreading geographically. Higher security redundancy and localisation requirements increase the strategic value of key hardware and intermediate goods. Markets such as Korea, positioned at the industrial interface of global compute infrastructure via semiconductors and critical electronics, are often seen as more direct equity expressions of the AI capex cycle. For portfolios, the value of this exposure is not only “faster growth”, but “more observable capex and more stable policy support”.

In addition, “defence and security” has returned to investors’ agendas for the first time since the end of the Cold War. Influenced by Trump’s “Donroeism” and the Russia–Ukraine war, both the US and Europe are placing defence higher on the priority list. The distinctive feature of defence assets is that demand does not come from marginal household consumption; it is closer to a fiscal function constrained by national security. Once budgets step up, the political resistance to reversing them is greater, so order visibility is typically stronger. This gives defence-related equities a more defensive allocation role in a regionalised world: when conflict and sanctions risk rise, they can add resilience at the portfolio level.

That said, defence-sector price sensitivity often runs ahead of fundamentals: event-driven repricing followed by mean reversion is common. A more robust framing is to treat it as a portfolio “tail insurance” or risk-hedging factor, rather than a linear-growth core holding. Its value lies in reducing drawdowns, not in guaranteeing outperformance every quarter.

Hong Kong equities and mainland China assets are another area worth considering. Labelling them simply as “cheap” is insufficient; their allocation value stems from two factors. First, pricing often bakes in pessimistic expectations early, leaving room for rebalancing. Second, their policy function and sector composition differ from those of US and European assets, potentially improving portfolio correlation structure. In the age of regionalisation, correlations do not automatically fall; they can rise during risk events. Structurally different assets can therefore provide more meaningful hedging.

Rates and Treasuries: Keep the Curve Steepening

The core tension in 2026 rates markets can be summarised in one line: the front end is more a function of the policy path, while the long end is more a container for term premia. Rate-cut expectations do help front-end yields decline, but whether the long end follows depends on whether inflation tail risks, fiscal supply pressure and political uncertainty allow term premia to keep compressing. In other words, long-end “stubbornness” may not mean markets have mispriced the number of cuts; it may mean markets are repricing long-run risk.

Supply dynamics amplify this structural difference. Changes in US fiscal funding composition directly affect supply–demand across maturities: the front end is easier to absorb when money markets have capacity. In contrast, the long end is more prone to pulse-like volatility driven by risk budgets and term premia. The portfolio implication is clear: duration exposure should be managed in layers, avoiding a one-path bet on “inflation fully disappearing and term premia returning to ultra-low levels”. Curve-structure trades (for instance, steepening strategies) persist not merely because of superior trading skill, but also because they align with the different pricing mechanisms of the front and long ends.

Crypto: Separate Accounting for “Digital Commodities” and Secondary Risk Assets

In 2026, the key for crypto is not simply “will it rise?”, but sharper internal differentiation. Bitcoin is more readily understood as a non-sovereign, rules-based supply asset that is portable across borders—a “digital commodity”. Under a regionalisation narrative, it is more likely to absorb demand for alternative payment systems and hedges. By contrast, a subset of tokens that behave more like equity-style risk assets are priced more on growth stories, ecosystem expansion and risk appetite. When risk-free yields remain attractive, regulation becomes clearer, and traditional capital markets offer more mature funding and exit channels, equity-like tokens must offer higher risk compensation to justify allocation.

As a result, crypto allocation is better approached via “separate books” rather than a single basket: place bitcoin in a commodity/alternative-asset framework, using small weights to obtain portfolio-level convexity; treat equity-like tokens as high-volatility risk assets with stricter return hurdles and clearer risk budgets. The core of the regionalisation era is not to embrace every new asset, but to identify which assets remain more explainable under the new constraints.

Use Hard-Constraint Assets as the Core, Use Structural Divergence as the Return Engine

Putting the above together, a 2026 portfolio looks more like managing a set of “hard constraints”: supply constraints restore the strategic role of commodities and resource equities; capex supports earnings visibility across the AI infrastructure chain; policy-driven orders enhance the resilience of defence and security; the return of term premia reshapes the distribution of duration returns; and select non-US assets provide reflexive hedging through valuation structure and policy functions.

This does not require perfect prediction of every event. On the contrary, the rarest skill in the age of regionalisation is to place the portfolio in a position that relies less on flawless forecasting: let hard assets and infrastructure absorb structural demand; let curve structures absorb structural divergence; and let hedging factors absorb structural noise. Trading in 2026 is no longer about “guessing the answer”, but about “acknowledging constraints”—and rewriting asset-allocation priorities accordingly.

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Humana: Market Is Right To Be Skeptical
data.description:
We began covering Humana Inc. (HUM) two years ago. At the time, we were skeptical that the long term story was intact and gave it a "Sell".

Our more recent coverage has been neutral as the company sported a mediocre valuation and had higher than average risks. Last time we wrote about it, we gave it a "hold" with the suggestion that the best way to make money, would be to use covered calls. We delve into the recent earnings and news and tell you where we stand.

The Big Picture

Humana is grappling with two big problems. The first being that their costs continue to go up faster than their revenues. This is reflected in their trailing 12 month profit margins.

They are struggling with rising expenses that keep moving faster than their revenues.

The second problem had to do with the unfortunate incident with their Medicare Advantage Star Ratings. These ratings determine the federal bonus payments Humans gets. A drop below 4 stars means losing a 5% revenue bonus per member, which was formerly flowing almost entirely to the bottom line. In 2024 (bonus for year 2025) about 93% of Humana's members were in 4 star or higher rated programs. In 2025 (bonus year 2026) this dropped to 25%. Humana claimed this was due to narrowly missing cutoff points on a single large contract which covers majority of its members. Humana even sued CMS for the alleged errors, but that hasn't worked out.

The Next Chapter

The bonus payment issue has been well known for a long time and the earnings estimates for 2026 reflect this.

The latest developments were on the pricing for 2026 revenue changes for Medicare Advantage. Two days back, CMS released the 2027 Advance Notice for Medicare Advantage payment rates. The proposal was a shock to the industry in general and Humana in particular. The "offering" was a net revenue increase of only 0.09%. While some may think that CMS came up with this rounding error through a random selection, there was some method to their madness.

The announcement includes a growth rate of underlying costs of 4.97%. This is offset by a -0.03% change in the Medicare Advantage Star Ratings for 2027 quality bonus payments, a -3.32% risk model revision and normalization of risk adjustment updates, plus a -1.53% change in sources of diagnoses.

Star rating updates include providing a list of eligible disasters for adjustment, non-substantive measure specification updates. It also means providing a list of measures included in the Part C and Part D improvement measures and Categorical Adjustment Index for the 2027 Star Ratings.

The proposals in the Advance Notice address coding differentials between Medicare Advantage and Original Medicare for CY 2027.

Diagnoses that are not reported or associated with a service would not be considered for risk adjustment.

The agency said its policies will promote greater payment accuracy, maintain beneficiary choice, help ensure affordable coverage and enable an even more stable MA program, even as some insurers have cut back their MA footprint due to challenges in the market.

Source: Health Care Finance News

Interestingly enough, CMS commented that it is well aware of how health insurers do something called "coding drift" and it expects their effective payments to actually go up a lot more.

"When considering estimated risk score trend in MA driven by coding practices and population changes, the expected average change in payments will be 2.54%," CMS said.

Source: Health Care Finance News

Impact

when you compare memberships in the Medicare Advantage plans as a percentage of total members, Humana stands out.

When you compare Medicare Advantage revenues as a percentage of total revenues, Humana looks like it could really get torpedoed. More than 84% of Humana's revenues comes from Medicare Advantage. So the impact is massive and it is even higher than UnitedHealth Group (UNH) which fell a similar percentage yesterday. What will likely happen is that ultimately there will be more "give" and the end result might not be that bad. Well, it might not look that bad. But 3% or 4% effective increase at a time of 5% plus cost inflation is likely to create further problems for Humana's dire margin rates.

Verdict

We would love to say this story is now set for great returns. Healthcare insurance never traded at high P/E multiples so you can always make the argument that they are "cheap", since the time these companies have traded. But understanding of the margin story is why we never bought HUM or UNH higher up. While the P/E looked low the price to sales was in the 90th percentile. Today we are close to the bottom tenth percentile and this is a time to start looking for opportunities.

In a rather expensive market, healthcare insurance looks like a rare place where you can perhaps make money by buying S&P 500 stocks. But it is not one where you go in with wild abandon. We will also note that both the 2008 and 2001 lows, formed at price to sales ratios significantly below today. So your choices are to go dollar cost averaging here or alternatively use covered calls and the very high implied volatility to create attractive risk-reward trades.

The January 2027 calls look modestly attractive and you can create a very good risk-adjusted entry here.

We haven't placed this trade but are on the lookout for opportunities in this sector.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: New Fortress Energy: Equity Expected To Survive - Continue Capturing Premium On Volatility
data.description:
Note to Investors:

This is my second article on New Fortress Energy (NFE), and serves as a follow up to the article, shown here. The articles should be read in succession to understand company-related developments since that point.

Background and Continued Strategy

Since my last coverage of New Fortress Energy, not unexpected to investors, there has been a lot of volatility in the share price.

As a reminder, the strategy I advised was one revolving around a heavy use of covered calls and cash secured puts, in order to capitalize on the rewarding option premiums this stock offers. In my personal portfolio, this strategy has worked well, with a cost basis nearing $0. This means, in theory, that my trade will soon be 'risk free.'

Based on recent restructuring news, which will highly likely result in common equity surviving, I foresee that the conditions are right to continue pursuing this strategy.

However, importantly, I would like to note that the restructuring news heavily lowers the risk-reward of the investment. Thus, while I would still like to reiterate my "Buy" rating on NFE, I recommend that investors cover most of their NFE position, as the outright long case appears to be less likely than during the publishing of my last article.

As mentioned previously, NFE is undoubtedly a play that carries heavy risk, but by continuing to capture option premium, investors can continue to lower their cost basis and come out nearly risk free, even in the worst case. By pursuing this strategy, I believe that investors can achieve favorable returns in both the bear and bull case.

Restructuring Update

On January 26th, a Bloomberg article was published revealing that Wes Edens, the CEO of NFE, is in talks with creditors to take preferred equity in NFE. Under this restructuring, creditors would take economic control of certain assets. These assets include:

  • Brazilian assets are expected to be taken under control by the 2029 senior secured notes bondholders. This includes CELBA 2, a 630 MW capacity power plant in Brazil, which is expected to start up shortly. Additional assets would include the 1.6 GW PortoCem facility, which is expected to start up in August of 2026.
  • Term loan holders are expected to be made whole through a recovery tied to a terminal in Puerto Rico and other downstream assets, which includes FLNG 1 in Mexico. However, the full context is unclear around the Puerto Rico business - and whether this includes the Puerto Rico long term contract, as well as the power generation facilities on the island.

The overall message to investors from this restructuring is clear - if preferred equity is to exist, then common equity will most likely survive as well. However, that doesn't mean that the risk of dilution is removed from the picture.

Despite this, seeing as how Wes Edens and bondholders aligned on UK restructuring, shows that some respect is given to common equity, which includes Edens himself through personal ownership and ownership through Fortress Investment Group. Both of these groups own a substantial amount of NFE common stock.

Most importantly, it is vital to remember that the deal is far from finalized - and conditions could change in either the positive or negative side. Depending on timing with FEMA, which I will discuss below, this may change the deal for the better.

FEMA Payment - And How It May Affect The Deal

In my previous article, this payment was mentioned as a catalyst that may be able to inject NFE with immediate liquidity, something that is deeply required at this point in time. To refresh investors, NFE has had a contract with the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) in order to supply Puerto Rico with power in order to recover from the power crisis the country faced after the 2017 Hurricane Maria. Management has stated that discussions were ongoing, and that the payment is expected at the end of 2025.

As of January 28th, no payment has been received. However, rumors have surfaced that seem to show that FEMA is closer than investors expect.

Indeed, looking deeper into the rumor, it does seem like there have been very credible developments, but that funds have not been distributed yet.

Looking at an official government source (usaspending.gov), a number of prime awards are shown for a company by the name of Weston Solutions. NFE has had extensive connections with Weston Solutions, as Weston was the contractor selected by USACE (US Army Corps of Engineers) delivering temporary generation projects in Puerto Rico. This is further confirmed by the company's press releases, where NFE is shown to have direct involvement.

USA Spending shows two new line items, at a combined ~$1.4B of awards. Jumping into some of the sub-awards behind the line items, we can see that NFE and it subsidiaries is clearly listed as part of this prime award.

Importantly, the website mentions that no payments ("outlays") have been made at this moment. However, this line item being added gives investors hope that the payment will go through.

Therefore, from the above information, it seems that the likelihood of the FEMA payment reaching NFE has substantially increased. Whether the payment is sent from FEMA or the DoD (Department of Defense) and routed through Weston does not matter. I believe these developments clearly show that something is in the works.

Of course, it is also important to note that even if a substantial payment was received, this would not immediately fix the liquidity issues that the firm is facing. However, this will give leverage in negotiations.

If FEMA was to come in before the deal is finalized, this could create positive developments surrounding restructuring, and allow for assets to be split in a way that is more favorable to shareholders. Knowing that there have been tangible developments very recently gives investors hope that this may occur before the deal is done.

If this was to not happen in time, it is possible that whoever gets economic ownership of the Puerto Rican assets, or possibly that it would be split between asset holders.

The above case is virtually the only case that I personally see for the outright long thesis - and to me it seems highly unlikely. Thus, I believe investors should consider covering most, around 80-90% of their position via options.

Forbearance Agreements

On November 18th, NFE signed a forbearance agreement with bondholders relating to the senior secured notes due 2029. The agreement lasted roughly a month.

Under this agreement, the due date for interest payments scheduled for November 17, 2025 has been effectively extended to December 15, 2025. During the forbearance period, NFE expects to continue to work constructively with the company’s stakeholders.

While the overall situation is dire, the fact that bondholders are willing to temporarily forego interest payments, signals that Chapter 7 bankruptcy is most definitely off the table. After all, if the bondholders believed that NFE's assets were better suited in their hands, they would immediately declare a early maturity event. In this case, NFE would be forced to pay off the entire remaining principal prematurely, and the company would not have the sufficient liquidity to perform this transaction.

After the expiry of this agreement, NFE extended the agreement, now until January 9th, 2026. More importantly, some language in the press release for this agreement has changed.

New Fortress Energy Inc. (Nasdaq: NFE) (“NFE” or the “Company”) today announced that it has extended its forbearance agreement with representatives of the holders of its new senior secured notes due 2029 from December 15, 2025 to January 9, 2026. During the forbearance period, NFE expects to continue to advance the completion of its restructuring with the company’s stakeholders.

Interestingly, since January 9th, the subsequent extension of the forbearance agreement that investors were waiting for still has not come, as of January 28th, 2026. Before any news of a potential restructuring deal was announced, Bloomberg has previously announced that NFE is in talks and discussing an extension to the agreement. This largely makes sense, as court proceedings may take several months more to finalize. I believe it is most likely that bondholders are currently in a "handshake agreement" with Wes Edens.

I encourage investors to keep imminent developments related to forbearance or restructuring in mind when forming a potential timeline for their investment decision.

Investment Thesis

So after all this - where does this leave NFE, and what should an investor do?

The most important thing an investor should understand is that a simple long position in the stock is a tough bet. Although potential reward is possibly still on the table, such as in the case of a more positive restructuring backed by an imminent FEMA payment, which allows shareholders to retain more assets than currently assumed, the firms liquidity issues are real and cannot be ignored. On top of this, I believe that the chance of this occurring is quite low, as it centers around timing. However, that doesn't mean that common equity should be written off.

As I stated in my last article, weekly options are the key to NFE. Since writing my last article, due to serious volatility, with the share price even gapping down below $1 per share, I have captured enough earnings premium to make my trade 'risk free.'

Before writing any options, investors must develop a "roadmap" in mind - "What would be the next event that NFE is expected to announce? How will this event drive share price and for how long?".

Of course, it is tough to make exact predictions on what will happen, but keeping a timeline in mind is key to the options strategy.

I believe investors should enter into NFE via weekly cash secured puts at periods of lower relative strength, such as after negative announcements surrounding restructuring, earnings and subsequent 10-K/10-Q filings. By entering at depressed prices, and capturing extensive premium while doing so (in the range of 600-800% annualized at times of peak volatility), investors are able to establish a low average cost basis and wait for positive events and volatility to significantly raise the share price.

Once volatility in the favorable direction begins, such as, for example, at the time just before writing this article, investors should seek to sell weekly covered calls on 80-90% of their position, once again capturing premiums. Since investors should be more than alright with letting a majority of their position be let go, I would even encourage in-the-money covered calls.

Being willing to capture premium and letting the position go is vital, as regardless of outcome, even in the most favorable situation, some degree of dilution is bound to happen at current liquidity conditions.

Risk

As with any strategy, an options-based strategy carries risk as well.

In theory, knowing that common equity is relatively safe establishes a floor where the book value of the currently-yet-unknown assets owned by shareholders will establish a minimum price for the stock. Since investors don't yet know what this floor looks like, cash-secured puts can be a lucrative but risky bet if unfavorable, if imminent restructuring was to come to fruition. Thus, to mitigate risk, I recommend that investors keep position sizes low.

Covered calls don't quite carry the same risk, as, if volatility is captured correctly, investors should be able to exit their positions at a profit after premium, assuming that covered calls are sold after favorable news is released.

The largest single risk to this options-centered investment thesis is the complete breakdown of negotiations, where bondholders declare an acceleration of repayments. However, knowing how far negotiations have already come, this risk is incredibly low.

Conclusion

Taken all information together, I believe investors should adopt an options-focused, trading mentality to NFE, utilizing covered calls during periods of relative strength and cash secured puts in periods of weakness. This mentality should be driven by a "roadmap" of catalysts and important events ahead.

These events include, but are not limited to:

1. Potential developments with FEMA payments

2. Restructuring developments, which include updates on the future expected capital structure, fate of common equity, and debt holder asset recovery decisions

Based on the above strategy, I reiterate a highly cautious Buy rating on NFE, conditional on ensuring that investors utilize options to capture premium during periods of peak volatility and keep uncovered long exposure minimal.

I intend to keep investors updated on the NFE story as developments ensue.

Market Commentary

Amid pronounced tariff, fiscal, monetary, and geopolitical developments, the U.S. bond market delivered strong performance in 2025. The Bloomberg U.S. Agg returned 7.3%, its best annual result since 2020, driven by declining interest rates and narrowing yield premiums within corporate bonds and, particularly in the fourth quarter, Agency[2] mortgage-backed securities (MBS).

Despite higher tariffs and the longest government shutdown in history, the U.S. economy remained resilient. Third quarter GDP grew at a surprising 4.3% annualized rate, the fastest pace in two years. However, labor market conditions cooled meaningfully. Nonfarm payrolls declined by 105,000 in October and added just 64,000 jobs in November, pushing the unemployment rate to a four-year high of 4.6% as reported in December. Inflation indicators continued to moderate, with November Core CPI at 2.6% year over year, though the shutdown complicates the reliability of that reading.

The Federal Reserve reduced short-term rates by 75 basis points (bps) in 2025, including two cuts in the fourth quarter, bringing total easing to 175 bps since September 2024. Over the past quarter and year, the Treasury yield curve steepened: short rates dropped in tandem with Fed rate cuts, while the long end rose slightly, reflecting ongoing inflation concerns plus fiscal and monetary policy uncertainty. Discussions at recent Fed meetings revealed divisions among members over balancing downside employment risks against lingering inflation concerns. With the policy rate now approaching neutral, Chair Powell signaled policymakers can wait for further clarity on economic data before further cuts.

Portfolio Strategy

Guided by our focus on security selection and valuation discipline, we added approximately five percentage points to the Fund’s high-quality securitized holdings during 2025 and made significant adjustments to the credit portfolio. Within securitized products, we continue to find value in prime auto and student loan asset-backed securities (ABS) as well as lower coupon Agency MBS. We believe these holdings enhance return potential across various interest rate and prepayment environments.

While the Fund's Credit sector weighting remained steady, we were highly active at the issuer and security level. We added seven new issuers, including Mars, Meta Platforms, and Synopsys, while reducing exposure to several holdings—particularly longer-maturity holdings—where valuations appeared stretched.[3] With credit spreads near historic lows, we stayed shorter in credit to help limit downside if spreads widen. The fundamental credit backdrop is constructive, but cracks in artificial intelligence (AI)-related demand, evolving trade/ tariff outcomes, or geopolitical flareups could reprice credit spreads.

The Fund's duration remained stable during the year, ending at 6.1 years versus 6.0 years for the Bloomberg U.S. Agg.[4] Looking ahead, we expect a backdrop of stable-to-declining inflation and interest rates, underpinned by moderating price pressures and still-resilient growth. AI-related productivity tailwinds are encouraging, but they coexist with emerging labor market softness, ongoing stresses for lower income households, and persistent concerns about long-term fiscal and debt sustainability.

Outlook

We are optimistic about the long-term return prospects for fixed income given reasonable starting nominal and real yield levels, and we are excited about the opportunity to actively manage portfolio exposures, particularly if volatility in credit markets picks up. Thank you for your continued confidence in Dodge & Cox.

There were no notable detractors during the period.

2025

There were no notable detractors during the period.
